grounding_note: Four separate disagreement rows split human raters between Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice and Real Property > Construction/Contractors for contractor disputes. The deciding facts the descriptions actually turned on — dollar amount relative to the small-claims cap and whether the user wants self-help coaching versus full representation — are facts users hold and can disclose.

scenarios.opening_query: A contractor took my deposit for a bathroom remodel and stopped showing up. I want my money back.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: The deposit was $28,000, far more than small claims court allows.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It was a $28,000 deposit — way over what small claims handles.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: the dollar amount in dispute relative to the small-claims limit
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The deposit was $3,200 and I just want coaching on filing the small claims case myself.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's $3,200 and I'd rather file in small claims myself if someone shows me how.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 29's exact pattern: user assumed small claims until the amount exceeded the cap.

scenarios.opening_query: The company that redid our kitchen left it half finished and won't answer us. How do we take them to court?
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: We're out about $45,000 between what we paid and what it will cost to fix.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Between what we paid and the repair bids, it's about $45,000.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: total amount at stake in the contractor dispute
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The unfinished part is worth about $2,500 and we want to handle small claims ourselves.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's only about $2,500 left undone — we just want to do small claims ourselves.
scenarios.vetting_note: 'How do we take them to court' reads as self-help; amount reroutes to full construction representation.

scenarios.opening_query: Our roofer used the wrong shingles and now the roof leaks into the nursery. He says it's not his problem.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: Replacing the roof properly and repairing the water damage is quoted at $31,000.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The quotes to redo the roof and fix the water damage come to $31,000.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: cost of repair and damages sought
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The patch and paint repair is about $1,800 and I'd rather file small claims myself than pay a lawyer.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The fix is about $1,800 — I'd just do small claims myself.
scenarios.vetting_note: Roof leak severity is invisible in the opening; the repair quote is the routing fact.

scenarios.opening_query: I paid a fence company in full last October and they never came back to finish the last two sections.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: They also filed a lien against my house claiming I owe them more money.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Now they've actually put a lien on my house saying I owe them more.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a construction lien has been filed on the property
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The unfinished sections are worth about $1,200 and I just need help preparing a small claims filing.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's about $1,200 of work — I just need help getting a small claims case ready.
scenarios.vetting_note: A recorded lien is a procedural fact (mechanism M3 flavor) that takes the case out of small-claims self-help; taxonomy lists liens under Construction/Contractors.

scenarios.opening_query: Handyman charged us for materials he never bought and did work so badly we had to hire someone else.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: The redo involved structural framing and cost $22,000; the CCB told us to get a construction attorney.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It turned into $22,000 of structural work — even the contractors board said get a construction attorney.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: scale of the defective work and any contractor-board proceedings
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: We're out about $900 total and just want to know how small claims works.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's about $900 — we just want to understand the small claims process.
scenarios.vetting_note: CCB referral detail comes straight from row 391's fact pattern.

scenarios.opening_query: The concrete guy poured our driveway wrong and it's already cracking. He blocked my number.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: Tearing out and repouring the driveway plus the damaged retaining wall is bid at $19,500.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The tear-out and repour with the retaining wall repair is bid at $19,500.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: repair cost relative to the small-claims ceiling
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: A skim-coat fix runs about $2,000 and I want to represent myself in small claims.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The fix is around $2,000 and I plan to represent myself.
scenarios.vetting_note: Identical grievance; only the disclosed repair economics change the right route.

scenarios.opening_query: We hired someone to build a deck. Half the boards are already warping and he wants his final payment.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: He's threatening to record a construction lien if we don't pay the final $12,000.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He's threatening to put a lien on the house over the last $12,000.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: lien threats and the size of the disputed balance
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The dispute is over his last $1,500 invoice and we're comfortable going to small claims ourselves.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's his last $1,500 bill — we're fine doing small claims on our own.
scenarios.vetting_note: Lien exposure plus five-figure balance is what the construction subcategory description is written for.

scenarios.opening_query: Contractor demolished our old porch, then disappeared for months. Now there's a hole in the front of my house.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: We already paid him $34,000 of the contract price before he vanished.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: We'd already paid him $34,000 before he disappeared.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: amount already paid to the vanished contractor
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: We only paid a $2,000 demolition deposit and want to claim it back ourselves in small claims.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He only got a $2,000 deposit — we want to claim that back ourselves.
scenarios.vetting_note: Abandonment cases arrive emotionally framed; the paid-in amount silently determines forum.

scenarios.opening_query: The painters we hired ruined our hardwood floors with spilled stain and refuse to pay for refinishing.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: Refinishing the floors throughout the main level is quoted at $16,000 and their insurer denied our claim.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The refinishing quote is $16,000 and their insurance company already denied us.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: damage amount and insurer involvement
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: One small landing needs refinishing, about $1,100, and I'd rather just file in small claims.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's one landing, about $1,100 — small claims is fine for that.
scenarios.vetting_note: Insurer denial detail parallels row 391 and pushes past self-help.

scenarios.opening_query: Our general contractor's subcontractor botched the carpet install and the GC says take it up with the sub.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: The whole remodel contract is $180,000 and the GC is withholding warranty work on the rest of the job over this dispute.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The remodel contract is $180,000 and now he's withholding warranty work on the whole job.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the dispute is a small standalone item or entangled with a large construction contract
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The carpet redo is $5,000, everything else is done and fine, and we want to pursue the sub in small claims ourselves.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's just the $5,000 carpet job — the rest is finished — and we'd do small claims ourselves.
scenarios.vetting_note: Directly built from rows 59/165 (the carpet-install scenario both raters saw twice).

scenarios.opening_query: I need a construction attorney to go after the contractor who did our siding wrong.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Construction/Contractors || Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice
scenarios.hidden_fact: The repair is only $2,300, so every lawyer says it's really a small claims case I should file myself.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a $2,300 repair — honestly I just need help filing small claims myself.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the amount justifies representation or small-claims self-help
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The siding failure caused rot and the total damage is $60,000.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The rot behind it brought the total to $60,000.
scenarios.vetting_note: Reverse direction: user over-asks for a construction litigator when the amount fits small claims coaching.

scenarios.opening_query: Looking for a lawyer to sue the landscaping company that killed our new lawn and shrubs.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Construction/Contractors || Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || General Litigation > Property Damage
scenarios.hidden_fact: The replacement plants come to about $1,900 and I mainly want advice on presenting my small claims case.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Replacing everything is about $1,900 — I really just want advice for small claims court.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: damage amount and whether the user wants coaching or representation
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Their grading work also cracked our irrigation main and patio; total damage is $27,000.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Their grading cracked the irrigation main and patio too — it's $27,000 all in.
scenarios.vetting_note: 'Sue them' language masks a coaching-sized dispute until the number is disclosed.

scenarios.opening_query: A remodeling company breached our contract and I want to take legal action against them.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Construction/Contractors || Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice
scenarios.hidden_fact: The breach is a $4,000 cabinet order they never delivered; I want to handle court myself with some guidance.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's the $4,000 cabinet order they never delivered — I can go to court myself with a little guidance.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: size and nature of the breach and the user's self-representation intent
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: They walked off a $95,000 addition at the framing stage and we need full representation.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: They abandoned our $95,000 addition half-framed — we need a lawyer on this.
scenarios.vetting_note: Formal 'breach of contract' language often overstates small disputes; amount discloses the real forum.

scenarios.opening_query: Need legal help with a dispute against the plumbing contractor who worked on our house last month.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Construction/Contractors || Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice
scenarios.hidden_fact: He overcharged us $1,400 beyond the estimate and I just want to know how to bring a small claims case.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He billed $1,400 over the estimate — I just want to know how small claims works.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: the amount in dispute and remedy the user wants
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: His unpermitted repipe flooded two floors; damages are around $38,000 and his insurer denied fault.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: His repipe flooded two floors — about $38,000, and his insurer denied our claim.
scenarios.vetting_note: Counterfactual is the row 391 flooded-plumbing pattern; intended fact is a coaching-scale overcharge.

scenarios.opening_query: I want to sue my contractor. He installed the wrong windows and won't make it right.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Construction/Contractors || Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice
scenarios.hidden_fact: The price difference on the two windows is $2,100 and I'd rather file in small claims than pay attorney fees.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a $2,100 difference on two windows — small claims makes more sense than paying a lawyer.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: dispute value versus cost of representation
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: All 22 windows are wrong and leaking; replacement is bid at $54,000.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's all 22 windows and they leak — $54,000 to replace.
scenarios.vetting_note: Scale of the same defect type flips the forum; user knows the number, intake doesn't.

scenarios.opening_query: My neighbor's tree guy dropped a limb through our fence and shed. He says his boss will handle it but nothing happens.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || General Litigation > Property Damage
scenarios.hidden_fact: The shed held my woodworking equipment; total damage is around $23,000 and their insurer is disputing it.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: My woodworking equipment was in that shed — about $23,000 total, and their insurance is fighting it.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: the total damage amount and insurer posture
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's about $1,600 in fence boards and shingles and I'm happy to file small claims myself.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's roughly $1,600 of fence and shingles — I can do small claims myself.
scenarios.vetting_note: Property-damage variant of the same amount boundary; both labels appear across rows 152/256 patterns.

scenarios.opening_query: An auto shop botched my transmission repair and the car is worse than when I brought it in.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Consumer Law > General || General Litigation > Property Damage
scenarios.hidden_fact: The dealer says the botched repair destroyed the transmission; I'm out $11,000 and they ignore my demand letters.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The dealership says the transmission is destroyed — I'm out $11,000 and the shop ignores my letters.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: the size of the loss and whether self-help has already failed
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The re-repair was $1,300 and I just want help preparing my small claims paperwork.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The redo cost $1,300 — I just need help with the small claims forms.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 152's auto-shop negligence; the loss size separates coaching from a consumer service-dispute case.

scenarios.opening_query: A moving company damaged our furniture and won't honor their own claims process. I want to escalate.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice
scenarios.hidden_fact: The damaged items total about $2,800 and I've decided to take them to small claims myself if someone explains the steps.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's about $2,800 of furniture — I'll take them to small claims myself if I know the steps.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: claim value and the user's intent to self-represent
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: They destroyed an antique collection appraised at $40,000 and their contract tries to cap liability at $500.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: They wrecked an antique collection worth $40,000 and claim a $500 liability cap.
scenarios.vetting_note: Escalation language sounds like litigation; disclosed value fits coaching.

scenarios.opening_query: Our builder never finished the punch list from our new house and now the warranty period is about to end.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: The punch list includes a failing foundation drain; bids to correct it run $26,000.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: One item is the foundation drain, which is failing — $26,000 to correct.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the open items are cosmetic or major structural work
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's paint touch-ups and a screen door, maybe $800, and I'd rather just file small claims before the deadline.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's touch-up paint and a screen door, about $800 — I'll just file small claims.
scenarios.vetting_note: Punch-list framing hides whether the defect is trivial or structural; the bid discloses it.

scenarios.opening_query: I have a dispute with the company that built our garage addition and I think we need to go to court.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Construction/Contractors || Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice
scenarios.hidden_fact: The only open issue is a $950 charge for materials they never used, and I plan to represent myself.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a $950 charge for materials they never used — I'm fine representing myself.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: size of the open dispute and self-representation plan
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The addition failed inspection for missing footings and correcting it is bid at $48,000.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It failed inspection — no footings — and the fix is $48,000.
scenarios.vetting_note: Closing mirror case; 'go to court' plus disclosed sub-cap amount lands in coaching.
